message.content: Adversaries may develop their own capabilities in-house to support various phases of their operations, including creating malware, exploits, and self-signed certificates. This process involves identifying development requirements and building tailored solutions, which can be more effective and exclusive than purchased or stolen tools. The development may require diverse skill sets, which can be sourced internally or through contractors, provided the adversary maintains control over the requirements and exclusivity. The impact of this technique includes enhanced operational effectiveness and potentially more sophisticated and targeted attacks. Common exploitation methods include custom malware development and specialized toolkits for spearphishing, as demonstrated by Kimsuky's creation of a mailing toolkit. Mitigation strategies involve robust threat intelligence to identify and anticipate custom-developed tools, network segmentation to limit the spread of bespoke malware, and continuous monitoring for unusual activity that may indicate the use of unique adversary-developed capabilities.
